message:
fix(pager): key global recovery fences and group-commit queues by canonical path

RECOVERY_FENCES and GROUP_COMMIT_QUEUES are process-wide HashMaps
that coordinate multiple Connections opening the *same physical
database file* — either to replay a busy/recovery decision fairly
across them, or to batch commits. Previously both maps keyed their
buckets by the raw `Path` the caller passed in.

That's buggy: two Connections can open the same file under different
spellings (relative vs. absolute, with/without trailing slash
components resolved by the OS, symlink hops, or differing working
directories), land in *different* HashMap buckets, and silently
operate without the coordination they were supposed to get. On a
WAL-heavy workload this manifests as two independent recovery
fences racing, or two group-commit queues serializing disjoint
subsets of the same file's pending commits.

Fix:

- New helper `shared_file_state_key(&Path) -> PathBuf`:
  `fs::canonicalize(db_path)` is the preferred answer. When that
  fails (the file hasn't been created yet on the first open, the
  parent directory isn't accessible, etc.) we fall back to
  `path.to_path_buf()` if the path is already absolute, otherwise
  `std::env::current_dir().join(path)`. As a last resort — if we
  can't even resolve the cwd — we keep the original path so at
  worst we're no worse than before.
- `recovery_fence_for_path` and `group_commit_queue_for_path` now
  build their map keys through this helper before the `.entry(...)`
  call. `remove_group_commit_queue` likewise canonicalizes before
  `.remove(&key)` so a Connection closed under a different spelling
  than the one it opened with still evicts its queue cleanly (the
  previous code would leak the queue in that case).
- Locked by a new mod-level test,
  `shared_file_state_key_matches_relative_and_absolute_spellings`,
  which asserts that a relative path and its cwd-joined absolute
  spelling hash to the same key. The test deliberately uses a
  non-existent probe filename so it exercises the fallback branch
  (canonicalize returns None) rather than whatever the filesystem
  happens to have today.

This is a behavior-preserving change for the common case (a single
spelling of the path) and a correctness fix for the multi-spelling
case.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
